跟住就有個總新聞主任殺出嚟回覆處長封email，話佢做咗主管多年覺得同事嘅身心健康先係最重要嘅，今次發生呢件事，新聞處應該由外人徹底審查件事，避免悲劇再發生。

DIS,

The news has saddened us all. Working in this department for over 20 years and as a unit head for over 10 years, good staff management is always key to helping colleagues cope with work stress. Against this background, I am obliged to tell you that the department’s grade management (from decision-making to operational arrangement), especially in recent years, should asap undergo a thorough review (covering interviews with at least staff rep or any colleagues who wish to voice their views) by an outsider who has no role in staff promotion. A fair and just assessment by a third party on the department’s grade management will definitely help boost staff morale and improve work efficiency. And most important of all, prevention is better than cure. Therefore, before the next tragedy happens, I now formally write to you to demand for such a review so that we can put an end to any existing problems and put in place better measures to ensure staff benefits - in particular mental health - are of the department’s top priority.

While I have planned to resign next year, I am most happy to stay for a year or two should the review require my input and work. Colleagues’ well-being always tops my agenda.

Katherine Kwong
CIO/DM

佢又俾咗封新嘅email我，係Katherine 寫俾副處長 內容好長唔Summarize 喇

DD1,

I understand that the email is not intended for me or for all. Hope this  can clear colleagues’ concerns why I have not - as usual - responded to it swiftly.

Before deciding my next step which hinges very much on the management’s response, I would like to highlight the following:

a. The underlying problem, in my humble opinion after years of observations, lies in the existing grade management system as well as the attitude/performance of grade manager(s) in human resource management and handling staff concerns in recent years. WHO and HOW are the crux of the problems. DCC has been in existence for years and forms part of the grade management system. If DCC - usually chaired by a DD - is good enough for gauging staff views, all colleagues in ISD should be working happily and there should be no tragedies - be they fatal or not, physical or mental. Are colleagues happy and have they gained satisfaction from work?

b. IOs have been asked to and trained to be able of multitasking. The grade manager(s) are in the COVID-19 battle. Non-directorate IO colleagues are also in the same battle. If we (or I as I cannot represent other IO colleagues to speak) are able to perform more than one task at one time as we having been doing all along, I trust the grade manager(s) - who have been appointed to lead the some 300-strong department because they are supposed to be more knowledgeable, more capable, more talented than other IOs - should have no problem in handling news issues and staff crisis at the same time.

c. Focusing on COVID-19 fight is important, but the loss of a colleague - the cause of which was reported to be related to work stress and the news has been extremely devastating to many, if not all - is equally or even more important. Staff really matters. Press secretaries cannot accomplish all their tasks without the full support of SPO colleagues who are not press secretaries’ subordinates, and the ISD management will not be able to present their deliverables to the senior echelon without the concerted efforts of the department’s IO and non-IO colleagues.

As I handled two staff cases relating to work stress in the last 10 years - both in the same team, my experience tells me that staff issues should be attended to seriously and promptly. COVID-19 should not hinder our powerful, capable grade leaders from showing their courage and determination in arranging an independent evaluation - which I am not aware of any such exercise on the directorate’s management performance in recent years at least  - immediately to fix problems and make improvements, aiming to rebuild staff’s trust and confidence, and to boost the record-low workplace morale promptly. Colleagues are under performance review annually by the management, so I do not see why the grade manager(s) and the grade management system cannot be put under scrutiny by a third party once every two years from 2020. Colleagues have been pushed to keep pace with times, take up numerous training courses and even work in multidisciplinary mode, I strongly believe that colleagues would like to know whether our grade manager(s) have kept themselves abreast of the times by a biennial health check of the grade management system.

We are all living and working in the 21st Century, not Qin Dynasty when there was only one voice. Grade manager(s) and staff are on equal footing when it comes to labour issues. It is time for a check and see whether our managers adhere to good people management practices.

Katherine

佢又俾咗封新嘅email我，係Katherine 寫俾副處長 內容好長唔Summarize 喇

DD1,

I understand that the email is not intended for me or for all. Hope this  can clear colleagues’ concerns why I have not - as usual - responded to it swiftly.

Before deciding my next step which hinges very much on the management’s response, I would like to highlight the following:

a. The underlying problem, in my humble opinion after years of observations, lies in the existing grade management system as well as the attitude/performance of grade manager(s) in human resource management and handling staff concerns in recent years. WHO and HOW are the crux of the problems. DCC has been in existence for years and forms part of the grade management system. If DCC - usually chaired by a DD - is good enough for gauging staff views, all colleagues in ISD should be working happily and there should be no tragedies - be they fatal or not, physical or mental. Are colleagues happy and have they gained satisfaction from work?

b. IOs have been asked to and trained to be able of multitasking. The grade manager(s) are in the COVID-19 battle. Non-directorate IO colleagues are also in the same battle. If we (or I as I cannot represent other IO colleagues to speak) are able to perform more than one task at one time as we having been doing all along, I trust the grade manager(s) - who have been appointed to lead the some 300-strong department because they are supposed to be more knowledgeable, more capable, more talented than other IOs - should have no problem in handling news issues and staff crisis at the same time.

c. Focusing on COVID-19 fight is important, but the loss of a colleague - the cause of which was reported to be related to work stress and the news has been extremely devastating to many, if not all - is equally or even more important. Staff really matters. Press secretaries cannot accomplish all their tasks without the full support of SPO colleagues who are not press secretaries’ subordinates, and the ISD management will not be able to present their deliverables to the senior echelon without the concerted efforts of the department’s IO and non-IO colleagues.

As I handled two staff cases relating to work stress in the last 10 years - both in the same team, my experience tells me that staff issues should be attended to seriously and promptly. COVID-19 should not hinder our powerful, capable grade leaders from showing their courage and determination in arranging an independent evaluation - which I am not aware of any such exercise on the directorate’s management performance in recent years at least  - immediately to fix problems and make improvements, aiming to rebuild staff’s trust and confidence, and to boost the record-low workplace morale promptly. Colleagues are under performance review annually by the management, so I do not see why the grade manager(s) and the grade management system cannot be put under scrutiny by a third party once every two years from 2020. Colleagues have been pushed to keep pace with times, take up numerous training courses and even work in multidisciplinary mode, I strongly believe that colleagues would like to know whether our grade manager(s) have kept themselves abreast of the times by a biennial health check of the grade management system.

We are all living and working in the 21st Century, not Qin Dynasty when there was only one voice. Grade manager(s) and staff are on equal footing when it comes to labour issues. It is time for a check and see whether our managers adhere to good people management practices.

Katherine

佢又俾咗封新嘅email我，係Katherine 寫俾副處長 內容好長唔Summarize 喇

DD1,

I understand that the email is not intended for me or for all. Hope this  can clear colleagues’ concerns why I have not - as usual - responded to it swiftly.

Before deciding my next step which hinges very much on the management’s response, I would like to highlight the following:

a. The underlying problem, in my humble opinion after years of observations, lies in the existing grade management system as well as the attitude/performance of grade manager(s) in human resource management and handling staff concerns in recent years. WHO and HOW are the crux of the problems. DCC has been in existence for years and forms part of the grade management system. If DCC - usually chaired by a DD - is good enough for gauging staff views, all colleagues in ISD should be working happily and there should be no tragedies - be they fatal or not, physical or mental. Are colleagues happy and have they gained satisfaction from work?

b. IOs have been asked to and trained to be able of multitasking. The grade manager(s) are in the COVID-19 battle. Non-directorate IO colleagues are also in the same battle. If we (or I as I cannot represent other IO colleagues to speak) are able to perform more than one task at one time as we having been doing all along, I trust the grade manager(s) - who have been appointed to lead the some 300-strong department because they are supposed to be more knowledgeable, more capable, more talented than other IOs - should have no problem in handling news issues and staff crisis at the same time.

c. Focusing on COVID-19 fight is important, but the loss of a colleague - the cause of which was reported to be related to work stress and the news has been extremely devastating to many, if not all - is equally or even more important. Staff really matters. Press secretaries cannot accomplish all their tasks without the full support of SPO colleagues who are not press secretaries’ subordinates, and the ISD management will not be able to present their deliverables to the senior echelon without the concerted efforts of the department’s IO and non-IO colleagues.

As I handled two staff cases relating to work stress in the last 10 years - both in the same team, my experience tells me that staff issues should be attended to seriously and promptly. COVID-19 should not hinder our powerful, capable grade leaders from showing their courage and determination in arranging an independent evaluation - which I am not aware of any such exercise on the directorate’s management performance in recent years at least  - immediately to fix problems and make improvements, aiming to rebuild staff’s trust and confidence, and to boost the record-low workplace morale promptly. Colleagues are under performance review annually by the management, so I do not see why the grade manager(s) and the grade management system cannot be put under scrutiny by a third party once every two years from 2020. Colleagues have been pushed to keep pace with times, take up numerous training courses and even work in multidisciplinary mode, I strongly believe that colleagues would like to know whether our grade manager(s) have kept themselves abreast of the times by a biennial health check of the grade management system.

We are all living and working in the 21st Century, not Qin Dynasty when there was only one voice. Grade manager(s) and staff are on equal footing when it comes to labour issues. It is time for a check and see whether our managers adhere to good people management practices.

Katherine
